description: 500 pre-1920 historic mines on BLM and USFS public land in the Rocky Mountain region. Covers major gold, silver, and copper districts across Colorado, Montana, Wyoming, and Idaho. All entries are from documented historic mining operations established before 1920 and located on publicly accessible federal land.

mines.name: Cresson Mine
mines.description: The Cresson Mine emerged from the search of hardened veterans who had already worked out a dozen smaller claims, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1894 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. Total recorded output reached $491,417 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Cresson Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Portland Mine
mines.description: The Portland Mine was located after a chance surface float of gold-bearing quartz caught a prospector's eye, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1902 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. Total recorded output reached $295,010 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Portland Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Independence Mine
mines.description: The Independence Mine was discovered by a group of forty-niners following the classic miner's instinct—pick up every likely rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1911 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. Total recorded output reached $211,403 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Independence Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Vindicator Mine
mines.description: The Vindicator Mine was discovered by a group of forty-niners following the classic miner's instinct—pick up every likely rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1903 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. Total recorded output reached $174,147 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Vindicator Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Isabella Mine
mines.description: The Isabella Mine was discovered by a group of forty-niners following the classic miner's instinct—pick up every likely rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1912 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Management secured contracts to supply a regional smelter, locking in favorable prices and driving expansion of the underground workings. A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. Total recorded output reached $145,448 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Isabella Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Victor Mine
mines.description: The Victor Mine came to light when a lone placer miner noticed the gleam of native gold in the creek gravel, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1891 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. Total recorded output reached $72,760 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Victor Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. mines.name: El Paso Mine
mines.description: The El Paso Mine was located after a chance surface float of gold-bearing quartz caught a prospector's eye, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1901 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. Total recorded output reached $159,606 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the El Paso Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Granite Mine
mines.description: The Granite Mine was located after a chance surface float of gold-bearing quartz caught a prospector's eye, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1895 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. Total recorded output reached $404,140 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Granite Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Pharmacist Mine
mines.description: The Pharmacist Mine emerged from the search of hardened veterans who had already worked out a dozen smaller claims, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1897 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. Total recorded output reached $80,875 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Pharmacist Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.description: The Anchoria-Leland Mine was staked after local ranchers found suspicious yellow glints in a dry wash near their pasture, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1909 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The owner, a self-made man who had arrived with nothing but a grubstake and an iron will, became one of the wealthiest citizens in the territory. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. Total recorded output reached $147,178 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Anchoria-Leland Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Elkton Mine
mines.description: The Elkton Mine was uncovered by a former sea captain who had traded his ship's compass for a miner's pick, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1918 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. Total recorded output reached $154,849 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Elkton Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

Total recorded output reached $264,311 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Galley Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Golden Cycle Mine
mines.description: The Golden Cycle Mine was uncovered by a former sea captain who had traded his ship's compass for a miner's pick, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1915 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Management secured contracts to supply a regional smelter, locking in favorable prices and driving expansion of the underground workings. A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. Total recorded output reached $212,918 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Golden Cycle Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: High Five Mine
mines.description: The High Five Mine was first staked by prospectors who traced a promising quartz vein up the mountain drainage, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1909 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. Total recorded output reached $99,074 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the High Five Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Junior Mine
mines.description: The Junior Mine was pegged when a winter camp cook noticed sparkling flecks in the rock used for his fireplace, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1916 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. Total recorded output reached $458,938 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Junior Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Kathleen Mine
mines.description: The Kathleen Mine was discovered by a group of forty-niners following the classic miner's instinct—pick up every likely rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1912 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. Total recorded output reached $284,653 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Kathleen Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Lida Mine
mines.description: The Lida Mine was staked after local ranchers found suspicious yellow glints in a dry wash near their pasture, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1905 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. Total recorded output reached $339,503 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Lida Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Lone Star Mine
mines.description: The Lone Star Mine was identified by a government geologist who recognized the telltale iron gossan capping a rich lode, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1891 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The owner, a self-made man who had arrived with nothing but a grubstake and an iron will, became one of the wealthiest citizens in the territory. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. Total recorded output reached $77,371 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Lone Star Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Morning Glory Mine
mines.description: The Morning Glory Mine was identified by a government geologist who recognized the telltale iron gossan capping a rich lode, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1905 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. Total recorded output reached $83,071 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Morning Glory Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.

At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. Total recorded output reached $296,760 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Night Hawk Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.

mines.name: Poverty Mine
mines.description: The Poverty Mine was identified by a government geologist who recognized the telltale iron gossan capping a rich lode, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1913 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. Total recorded output reached $101,486 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Poverty Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.

The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. Total recorded output reached $92,789 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Silver Dollar Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.

mines.name: Snowbird Mine

mines.name: Tidal Wave Mine
mines.description: The Tidal Wave Mine was discovered by a group of forty-niners following the classic miner's instinct—pick up every likely rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1917 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. Total recorded output reached $88,414 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Tidal Wave Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

mines.name: Two Bears Mine
mines.description: The Two Bears Mine came to notice when a surveying party camped on the hillside and examined the local float rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1914 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. Total recorded output reached $217,691 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Two Bears Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Union Mine
mines.description: The Union Mine was located after a chance surface float of gold-bearing quartz caught a prospector's eye, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1895 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Cripple Creek district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The owner, a self-made man who had arrived with nothing but a grubstake and an iron will, became one of the wealthiest citizens in the territory. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. Total recorded output reached $300,367 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Union Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.

mines.name: Chrysolite Mine
mines.description: The Chrysolite Mine came to light after a storm washed away hillside debris and exposed a wide streak of galena, launching silver mining operations in 1895 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. Total recorded output reached $416,230 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Chrysolite Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Iron Silver Mine
mines.description: The Iron Silver Mine was located after a prospector noticed the blue-black tarnish of argentite in a canyon wall, launching silver mining operations in 1883 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. Total recorded output reached $276,064 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Iron Silver Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.

Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. Total recorded output reached $401,496 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Ibex Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. mines.description: The Boss Mine came to light after a storm washed away hillside debris and exposed a wide streak of galena, launching silver mining operations in 1886 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The owner, a self-made man who had arrived with nothing but a grubstake and an iron will, became one of the wealthiest citizens in the territory. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. Total recorded output reached $397,140 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Boss Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Breece Iron Mine
mines.description: The Breece Iron Mine came to light after a storm washed away hillside debris and exposed a wide streak of galena, launching silver mining operations in 1902 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. Total recorded output reached $118,968 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Breece Iron Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Carbonate King Mine
mines.description: The Carbonate King Mine was staked by a greenhorn who mistook the heavy gray ore for lead—until the assay came back, launching silver mining operations in 1878 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. Total recorded output reached $131,384 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Carbonate King Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.

mines.name: Centennial Mine
mines.description: The Centennial Mine was found when a shepherd noticed his sheep avoided a particular hillside—the scent of sulfurous ore drove them off, launching silver mining operations in 1903 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. Total recorded output reached $334,042 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Centennial Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Chicago Mine
mines.description: The Chicago Mine emerged from careful float sampling by a Denver assayer who spent two seasons tracing the vein uphill, launching silver mining operations in 1887 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. Total recorded output reached $287,230 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Chicago Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Clara Mine
mines.description: The Clara Mine was identified when a passing prospector recognized the characteristic smell of sulfur near the outcrop, launching silver mining operations in 1897 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. Total recorded output reached $515,028 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Clara Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Coliseum Mine
mines.description: The Coliseum Mine emerged when a freighter's mule kicked loose a chunk of ore that assayed three hundred ounces silver per ton, launching silver mining operations in 1913 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. Total recorded output reached $413,885 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Coliseum Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Colonel Sellers Mine
mines.description: The Colonel Sellers Mine was discovered by Mexican prospectors following a century-old Spanish trail to a recorded silver source, launching silver mining operations in 1880 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. Total recorded output reached $167,125 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Colonel Sellers Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

mines.name: Denver City Mine
mines.description: The Denver City Mine was traced to outcropping gray carbonate ore that old-timers called 'black silver', launching silver mining operations in 1904 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. Total recorded output reached $351,971 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Denver City Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Evening Star Mine
mines.description: The Evening Star Mine was staked by a greenhorn who mistook the heavy gray ore for lead—until the assay came back, launching silver mining operations in 1884 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. Total recorded output reached $238,618 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Evening Star Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.

mines.name: Fifth Wheel Mine
mines.description: The Fifth Wheel Mine emerged from careful float sampling by a Denver assayer who spent two seasons tracing the vein uphill, launching silver mining operations in 1902 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Management secured contracts to supply a regional smelter, locking in favorable prices and driving expansion of the underground workings. A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. Total recorded output reached $631,290 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Fifth Wheel Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Forest Queen Mine
mines.description: The Forest Queen Mine was located after a prospector noticed the blue-black tarnish of argentite in a canyon wall, launching silver mining operations in 1880 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. Total recorded output reached $84,305 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Forest Queen Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.description: The Horseshoe Mine was found when a shepherd noticed his sheep avoided a particular hillside—the scent of sulfurous ore drove them off, launching silver mining operations in 1879 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. Total recorded output reached $324,283 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Horseshoe Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Ida Mine
mines.description: The Ida Mine was identified when a passing prospector recognized the characteristic smell of sulfur near the outcrop, launching silver mining operations in 1906 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. Total recorded output reached $595,851 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Ida Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: John Jay Mine
mines.description: The John Jay Mine emerged when a freighter's mule kicked loose a chunk of ore that assayed three hundred ounces silver per ton, launching silver mining operations in 1897 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. Total recorded output reached $402,425 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the John Jay Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Kansas Mine
mines.description: The Kansas Mine emerged when a freighter's mule kicked loose a chunk of ore that assayed three hundred ounces silver per ton, launching silver mining operations in 1913 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. Total recorded output reached $306,477 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Kansas Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: King Solomon Mine
mines.description: The King Solomon Mine was found when a shepherd noticed his sheep avoided a particular hillside—the scent of sulfurous ore drove them off, launching silver mining operations in 1903 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. Total recorded output reached $242,129 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the King Solomon Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: La Plata Mine
mines.description: The La Plata Mine was uncovered when a road-grading crew blasted through a hillside and hit a lens of rich cerargyrite, launching silver mining operations in 1902 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. Total recorded output reached $91,680 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the La Plata Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Lead Chief Mine
mines.description: The Lead Chief Mine was located after a prospector noticed the blue-black tarnish of argentite in a canyon wall, launching silver mining operations in 1906 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. Total recorded output reached $545,935 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Lead Chief Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Matchless Mine
mines.description: The Matchless Mine emerged from careful float sampling by a Denver assayer who spent two seasons tracing the vein uphill, launching silver mining operations in 1900 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Leadville district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. Total recorded output reached $129,353 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Matchless Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Smuggler-Union Mine
mines.description: The Smuggler-Union Mine was identified by a government geologist who recognized the telltale iron gossan capping a rich lode, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1884 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Telluride district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The owner, a self-made man who had arrived with nothing but a grubstake and an iron will, became one of the wealthiest citizens in the territory. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. Total recorded output reached $176,792 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Smuggler-Union Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Tomboy Mine
mines.description: The Tomboy Mine emerged from the search of hardened veterans who had already worked out a dozen smaller claims, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1915 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Telluride district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. Total recorded output reached $252,216 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Tomboy Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Iowa Mine
mines.description: The Iowa Mine came to light after a storm washed away hillside debris and exposed a wide streak of galena, launching silver mining operations in 1895 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Silverton district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. Total recorded output reached $366,453 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Iowa Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.

mines.name: Grand View Mine
mines.description: The Grand View Mine was found when a shepherd noticed his sheep avoided a particular hillside—the scent of sulfurous ore drove them off, launching silver mining operations in 1884 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Silverton district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. Total recorded output reached $519,206 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Grand View Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.

mines.name: Alpha Mine
mines.description: The Alpha Mine was identified when a passing prospector recognized the characteristic smell of sulfur near the outcrop, launching silver mining operations in 1897 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Creede district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. Total recorded output reached $319,993 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Alpha Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Arizona Mine
mines.description: The Arizona Mine was discovered by Mexican prospectors following a century-old Spanish trail to a recorded silver source, launching silver mining operations in 1894 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Creede district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. Total recorded output reached $61,606 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Arizona Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.

mines.description: The Belmont Mine was uncovered when a road-grading crew blasted through a hillside and hit a lens of rich cerargyrite, launching silver mining operations in 1907 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Georgetown district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The owner, a self-made man who had arrived with nothing but a grubstake and an iron will, became one of the wealthiest citizens in the territory. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. Total recorded output reached $164,737 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Belmont Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Colorado Central Mine
mines.description: The Colorado Central Mine was found when a shepherd noticed his sheep avoided a particular hillside—the scent of sulfurous ore drove them off, launching silver mining operations in 1887 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Georgetown district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. Total recorded output reached $72,121 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Colorado Central Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Dives-Pelican Mine
mines.description: The Dives-Pelican Mine was discovered by Mexican prospectors following a century-old Spanish trail to a recorded silver source, launching silver mining operations in 1874 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the Georgetown district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. Total recorded output reached $85,704 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Dives-Pelican Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

mines.name: Free America Mine
mines.description: The Free America Mine emerged when a freighter's mule kicked loose a chunk of ore that assayed three hundred ounces silver per ton, launching silver mining operations in 1871 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the Georgetown district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Management secured contracts to supply a regional smelter, locking in favorable prices and driving expansion of the underground workings. A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. Total recorded output reached $96,211 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Free America Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Green Lake Mine
mines.description: The Green Lake Mine was uncovered when a road-grading crew blasted through a hillside and hit a lens of rich cerargyrite, launching silver mining operations in 1895 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Georgetown district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. Total recorded output reached $207,199 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Green Lake Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Griffith Mine
mines.description: The Griffith Mine was located after a prospector noticed the blue-black tarnish of argentite in a canyon wall, launching silver mining operations in 1888 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Georgetown district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. Total recorded output reached $168,499 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Griffith Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Bates Hunter Mine
mines.description: The Bates Hunter Mine came to notice when a surveying party camped on the hillside and examined the local float rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1866 amid the rush and optimism of the Civil War era. Located in the Central City district of Colorado, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. Total recorded output reached $104,840 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Bates Hunter Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Portland-Mackey Mine

mines.name: Anaconda Mine
mines.description: The Anaconda Mine was located by prospectors following old reports of 'green rocks' used by indigenous peoples as paint pigment, launching copper mining operations in 1866 amid the rush and optimism of the Civil War era. Located in the Butte district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. Located in the Butte district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. mines.name: California Mine

Located in the Butte district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.
