mines.name: Copper King Mine
mines.description: The Copper King Mine was staked after Native American guides showed early explorers copper stains on canyon walls, launching copper mining operations in 1876 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the Butte district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. Total recorded output reached $1,074,369 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Copper King Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

mines.description: The Mike Horse Mine was identified by its brilliant blue-green gossan, visible from the valley floor, launching copper mining operations in 1883 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Butte district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. Located in the Butte district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. Total recorded output reached $568,728 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Minnie Healey Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.

mines.name: Henry Mine
mines.description: The Henry Mine was staked after local ranchers found suspicious yellow glints in a dry wash near their pasture, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1876 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the Virginia City district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. Total recorded output reached $318,982 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Henry Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Jackson Mine

Located in the Virginia City district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. Total recorded output reached $278,791 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Jackson Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.

Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. Total recorded output reached $88,344 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Jefferson Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. mines.name: Johnson Mine
mines.description: The Johnson Mine emerged from the search of hardened veterans who had already worked out a dozen smaller claims, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1908 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Virginia City district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Management secured contracts to supply a regional smelter, locking in favorable prices and driving expansion of the underground workings. A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. Total recorded output reached $164,631 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Johnson Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: June Bug Mine
mines.description: The June Bug Mine was first staked by prospectors who traced a promising quartz vein up the mountain drainage, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1868 amid the rush and optimism of the Civil War era. Located in the Virginia City district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. Total recorded output reached $266,944 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the June Bug Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

mines.name: Kirk Bannack Mine
mines.description: The Kirk Bannack Mine came to notice when a surveying party camped on the hillside and examined the local float rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1887 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Bannack district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. Total recorded output reached $209,927 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Kirk Bannack Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.

mines.name: Silver Bow Mine
mines.description: The Silver Bow Mine came to light when a lone placer miner noticed the gleam of native gold in the creek gravel, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1873 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the Bannack district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. Total recorded output reached $168,201 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Silver Bow Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.

mines.name: Carbonate Mine
mines.description: The Carbonate Mine was staked after local ranchers found suspicious yellow glints in a dry wash near their pasture, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1868 amid the rush and optimism of the Civil War era. Located in the Garnet district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. Total recorded output reached $103,139 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Carbonate Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Feeny Mine
mines.description: The Feeny Mine was uncovered by a former sea captain who had traded his ship's compass for a miner's pick, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1910 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the Garnet district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. Total recorded output reached $42,547 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Feeny Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. Total recorded output reached $171,737 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Buckley Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.

mines.name: Capital Mine
mines.description: The Capital Mine was staked after local ranchers found suspicious yellow glints in a dry wash near their pasture, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1900 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Helena district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. Total recorded output reached $122,483 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Capital Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Columbia Mine
mines.description: The Columbia Mine was discovered by a group of forty-niners following the classic miner's instinct—pick up every likely rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1904 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Helena district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. Total recorded output reached $41,360 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Columbia Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.

mines.name: Comstock Mine
mines.description: The Comstock Mine was uncovered by a former sea captain who had traded his ship's compass for a miner's pick, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1904 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Helena district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. Total recorded output reached $149,571 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Comstock Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

Located in the Helena district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. mines.name: Galena Mine
mines.description: The Galena Mine was located after a chance surface float of gold-bearing quartz caught a prospector's eye, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1907 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Helena district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. Total recorded output reached $120,279 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Galena Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Granite Mine
mines.description: The Granite Mine was uncovered by a former sea captain who had traded his ship's compass for a miner's pick, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1893 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Helena district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. Total recorded output reached $50,324 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Granite Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Green Gulch Mine
mines.description: The Green Gulch Mine was located after a chance surface float of gold-bearing quartz caught a prospector's eye, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1867 amid the rush and optimism of the Civil War era. Located in the Helena district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. Total recorded output reached $68,197 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Green Gulch Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.

mines.name: Montana Consolidated Mine
mines.description: The Montana Consolidated Mine was first staked by prospectors who traced a promising quartz vein up the mountain drainage, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1909 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Helena district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. Total recorded output reached $102,056 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Montana Consolidated Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.

In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. Total recorded output reached $143,154 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Elkhorn Belle Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Elkhorn Bullion Mine
mines.description: The Elkhorn Bullion Mine was staked by a greenhorn who mistook the heavy gray ore for lead—until the assay came back, launching silver mining operations in 1904 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Elkhorn district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. Total recorded output reached $82,154 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Elkhorn Bullion Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Elkhorn Chief Mine
mines.description: The Elkhorn Chief Mine was traced to outcropping gray carbonate ore that old-timers called 'black silver', launching silver mining operations in 1887 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Elkhorn district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. Total recorded output reached $51,893 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Elkhorn Chief Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Elkhorn Crown Mine
mines.description: The Elkhorn Crown Mine emerged from careful float sampling by a Denver assayer who spent two seasons tracing the vein uphill, launching silver mining operations in 1894 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Elkhorn district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. Total recorded output reached $31,082 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Elkhorn Crown Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Elkhorn Eagle Mine
mines.description: The Elkhorn Eagle Mine was staked by a greenhorn who mistook the heavy gray ore for lead—until the assay came back, launching silver mining operations in 1890 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Elkhorn district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. Total recorded output reached $86,862 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Elkhorn Eagle Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Elkhorn Gold Mine
mines.description: The Elkhorn Gold Mine was staked by a greenhorn who mistook the heavy gray ore for lead—until the assay came back, launching silver mining operations in 1902 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Elkhorn district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. Total recorded output reached $60,719 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Elkhorn Gold Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Elkhorn King Mine
mines.description: The Elkhorn King Mine was uncovered when a road-grading crew blasted through a hillside and hit a lens of rich cerargyrite, launching silver mining operations in 1872 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the Elkhorn district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The owner, a self-made man who had arrived with nothing but a grubstake and an iron will, became one of the wealthiest citizens in the territory. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. Total recorded output reached $130,897 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Elkhorn King Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Elkhorn Queen Mine
mines.description: The Elkhorn Queen Mine was staked by a greenhorn who mistook the heavy gray ore for lead—until the assay came back, launching silver mining operations in 1899 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Elkhorn district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. Total recorded output reached $41,742 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Elkhorn Queen Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Elkhorn Silver Mine
mines.description: The Elkhorn Silver Mine emerged when a freighter's mule kicked loose a chunk of ore that assayed three hundred ounces silver per ton, launching silver mining operations in 1888 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the Elkhorn district of Montana, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Management secured contracts to supply a regional smelter, locking in favorable prices and driving expansion of the underground workings. A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. Total recorded output reached $115,939 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Elkhorn Silver Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Duncan Mine
mines.description: The Duncan Mine came to notice when a surveying party camped on the hillside and examined the local float rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1902 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the South Pass district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. Total recorded output reached $36,645 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Duncan Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Emma Mine
mines.description: The Emma Mine was discovered by a group of forty-niners following the classic miner's instinct—pick up every likely rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1891 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the South Pass district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. Total recorded output reached $52,819 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Emma Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Flagstaff Mine
mines.description: The Flagstaff Mine was staked after local ranchers found suspicious yellow glints in a dry wash near their pasture, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1906 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the South Pass district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. Total recorded output reached $66,030 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Flagstaff Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.description: The Oregon Buttes Mine was pegged when a winter camp cook noticed sparkling flecks in the rock used for his fireplace, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1892 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the South Pass district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. Total recorded output reached $89,296 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Oregon Buttes Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Pioneer Mine
mines.description: The Pioneer Mine was uncovered by a former sea captain who had traded his ship's compass for a miner's pick, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1886 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in the South Pass district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. Total recorded output reached $59,529 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Pioneer Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Atlantic City Mine
mines.description: The Atlantic City Mine came to light when a lone placer miner noticed the gleam of native gold in the creek gravel, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1900 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Atlantic City district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. Total recorded output reached $61,697 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Atlantic City Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Buckeye Mine
mines.description: The Buckeye Mine came to notice when a surveying party camped on the hillside and examined the local float rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1902 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Atlantic City district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. Total recorded output reached $41,391 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Buckeye Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Burr Mine
mines.description: The Burr Mine was identified by a government geologist who recognized the telltale iron gossan capping a rich lode, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1904 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Atlantic City district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The owner, a self-made man who had arrived with nothing but a grubstake and an iron will, became one of the wealthiest citizens in the territory. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. Total recorded output reached $29,987 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Burr Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Continental Mine
mines.description: The Continental Mine emerged from the search of hardened veterans who had already worked out a dozen smaller claims, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1869 amid the rush and optimism of the Civil War era. Located in the Atlantic City district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. Total recorded output reached $34,101 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Continental Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Iron Ore Mine
mines.description: The Iron Ore Mine was identified by a government geologist who recognized the telltale iron gossan capping a rich lode, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1871 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the Atlantic City district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. Total recorded output reached $47,011 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Iron Ore Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Lincoln Mine
mines.description: The Lincoln Mine was uncovered by a former sea captain who had traded his ship's compass for a miner's pick, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1907 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Atlantic City district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. Total recorded output reached $58,476 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Lincoln Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Battle Creek Mine
mines.description: The Battle Creek Mine was staked after Native American guides showed early explorers copper stains on canyon walls, launching copper mining operations in 1906 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Encampment district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. Total recorded output reached $95,856 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Battle Creek Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Ben Holt Mine
mines.description: The Ben Holt Mine came to light when a miner hauling firewood noticed the vivid green malachite on a ledge above the trail, launching copper mining operations in 1898 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Encampment district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. Total recorded output reached $35,263 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Ben Holt Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Copperkettle Mine
mines.description: The Copperkettle Mine was found when early settlers dug a well and struck a vein of solid native copper twelve feet down, launching copper mining operations in 1904 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Encampment district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. By the time operations wound down, the Copperkettle Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Grand Encampment Mine
mines.description: The Grand Encampment Mine was discovered by a veteran Cornish miner who said the ground smelled like the old copper country at home, launching copper mining operations in 1899 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Encampment district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. Total recorded output reached $53,037 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Grand Encampment Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Hack Mine
mines.description: The Hack Mine was identified by its brilliant blue-green gossan, visible from the valley floor, launching copper mining operations in 1900 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Encampment district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Management secured contracts to supply a regional smelter, locking in favorable prices and driving expansion of the underground workings. A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. Total recorded output reached $52,830 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Hack Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Centennial Star Mine
mines.description: The Centennial Star Mine was located after a chance surface float of gold-bearing quartz caught a prospector's eye, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1892 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Centennial district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. Total recorded output reached $27,522 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Centennial Star Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.

mines.name: Atlanta Mine
mines.description: The Atlanta Mine emerged from the search of hardened veterans who had already worked out a dozen smaller claims, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1874 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the Idaho City district of Idaho, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. Total recorded output reached $168,601 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Atlanta Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

Located in the Idaho City district of Idaho, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. Total recorded output reached $166,223 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Boulder Creek Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

mines.name: Chatcolet Mine
mines.description: The Chatcolet Mine was discovered by a group of forty-niners following the classic miner's instinct—pick up every likely rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1907 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Idaho City district of Idaho, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. Total recorded output reached $56,092 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Chatcolet Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.

By the time operations wound down, the Clear Creek Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. mines.name: Kaiser Mine

Located in the Idaho City district of Idaho, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. Total recorded output reached $123,396 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Kaiser Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.

By the time operations wound down, the Florida Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. mines.name: Morning Star Mine

Located in the Silver City district of Idaho, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. Total recorded output reached $115,941 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Morning Star Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.

mines.name: Poorman Mine
mines.description: The Poorman Mine was staked by a greenhorn who mistook the heavy gray ore for lead—until the assay came back, launching silver mining operations in 1904 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Silver City district of Idaho, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. By the time operations wound down, the Poorman Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.

mines.name: Gold Finch Mine
mines.description: The Gold Finch Mine was located after a chance surface float of gold-bearing quartz caught a prospector's eye, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1864 amid the rush and optimism of the Civil War era. Located in the Warren district of Idaho, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. Total recorded output reached $83,492 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Gold Finch Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: North Star Mine
mines.description: The North Star Mine came to notice when a surveying party camped on the hillside and examined the local float rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1864 amid the rush and optimism of the Civil War era. Located in the Warren district of Idaho, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. Total recorded output reached $89,788 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the North Star Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.
